title: Hongwu's Founding
subtitle: the peasant orphan who became emperor and built the most autocratic dynasty in Chinese history
bullets: Zhu Yuanzhang — youngest son of a destitute Anhui tenant farmer, orphaned at sixteen by the famine that killed his parents and brother in a few weeks, four-year mendicant Buddhist novice, Red Turban warlord — proclaimed the Ming dynasty at Nanjing in January 1368 as the Hongwu Emperor.
He is the only Chinese founding emperor of pure peasant birth in the entire imperial period, and his thirty-year reign was one of the most autocratic and violent in Chinese history.

On the night of September 14, 1368, the last Yuan emperor Toghon Temur abandoned the capital Khanbaliq and fled north into Mongolia; the next morning the Ming general Xu Da entered the city without opposition, ending the Mongol period.

After the Hu Weiyong purge of 1380 — in which Hongwu executed his Chief Minister and an estimated 30,000 alleged accomplices — the emperor abolished the office of Chief Minister entirely, concentrating administrative power in the throne to a degree unmatched in any earlier Chinese dynasty.

The lijia rural-control system grouped every 110 households into a li under rotating leadership of the ten wealthiest, codified household occupations as hereditary, and tied Chinese peasants to their villages with limited rights of mobility.

The Hongwu Code of 1397 was the most comprehensive Chinese legal code since the Tang and legalized flaying alive for corruption above 60 ounces of silver — the punishment to be administered to the corrupt official in his own former office.

Hongwu officially distrusted eunuchs and inscribed an iron tablet at the palace forbidding them from holding political office or learning to read, but his Twenty-Four Departments of imperial household staff would within a generation become the parallel administration that ran the Ming for the next 270 years.

The 1393 Lan Yu purge eliminated almost all the surviving senior generals of the Ming founding to clear the way for Hongwu's grandson to inherit a throne with no rival power centers — a strategy that backfired when one of Hongwu's own sons, the Prince of Yan, used his army to overthrow the legitimate succession four years after Hongwu's death.

title: Yongle and the Forbidden City
subtitle: the usurper emperor who built Beijing, the Forbidden City, and the largest reference work in pre-modern history
bullets: Hongwu's fourth son the Prince of Yan refused to accept the demotion his nephew the Jianwen Emperor had ordered, gathered his veteran northern army, and won the four-year Jingnan Campaign civil war by capturing Nanjing in July 1402; the Jianwen Emperor disappeared in the burning palace and his fate was never officially confirmed.

The new Yongle Emperor consolidated power with brutal purges of officials loyal to his nephew — including the scholar Fang Xiaoru, whose nine clans plus a tenth clan of his students were executed, an estimated 873 people total — but then began the most ambitious building program of the Ming.

Yongle moved the capital from Nanjing to Beijing, formally relocating in 1421; the decision committed the Ming to defending a capital 700 miles from its tax base in the Yangtze valley and shaped Ming policy for the next 240 years.

The Forbidden City, constructed between 1406 and 1420 with roughly a million laborers, contains 980 buildings on a 180-acre site behind a 26-foot wall and 170-foot moat and remains the largest preserved palace complex on Earth.

The Yongle Encyclopedia ran to 11,095 volumes containing roughly 370 million Chinese characters — the largest reference work in human history before Wikipedia, several orders of magnitude larger than any contemporary European or Islamic compilation.

Most of the Yongle Encyclopedia is now lost; only about 400 of the original 11,095 volumes survive, scattered across libraries from Beijing to Cambridge to several American universities after the Anglo-French sack of 1860 and the Boxer Rebellion looting of 1900.

Yongle led five personal campaigns into the Mongolian steppe between 1410 and 1424, projecting Chinese military power further than any post-Yuan Chinese ruler — but the steppe was too vast for permanent occupation and Yongle himself died on the return from his fifth campaign in 1424.

The Temple of Heaven, completed in 1420, with its Hall of Prayer for Good Harvests built without a single nail on a three-tiered marble platform, was the second great monument of Yongle's Beijing building program.

title: Zheng He's Treasure Fleet
subtitle: seven gigantic naval expeditions to East Africa, ninety years before Vasco da Gama, then the program was abandoned forever
bullets: Between 1405 and 1433 the Muslim eunuch admiral Zheng He led seven naval expeditions across the Indian Ocean — to Java, India, Sri Lanka, the Persian Gulf, Arabia, and the East African coast at Malindi and Mogadishu — each fleet carrying roughly 28,000 men aboard approximately 300 ships.

Zheng He was born Ma He, the son of a Hui Muslim family in Yunnan; captured at age ten when Hongwu's army conquered Yunnan, castrated and made an imperial servant, he rose to become the Yongle Emperor's most trusted associate and commander of the largest naval expeditions in pre-modern history.

Ming chronicles describe the largest treasure ships as up to 137 meters (450 feet) long with nine masts and four decks; modern naval historians dispute whether ships of that scale were structurally feasible but agree the treasure ships were vastly larger than anything Europe could build for another two centuries.

The fourth voyage returned to China in 1415 with a giraffe brought from the Swahili port of Malindi in modern Kenya, identified by Ming officials with the qilin — the mythical Chinese creature whose appearance heralded the reign of a sage-ruler — making it useful political propaganda for the usurper-emperor.

The persistent claim that Ming ships reached the Americas in 1421, popularized by Gavin Menzies's bestselling book, has been comprehensively rejected by professional historians; the Menzies book is bunk and there is no evidence any Ming vessel ever crossed the Pacific or rounded Africa.

Zheng He died at sea around 1433 on the return from the seventh voyage; the Confucian-aligned faction at court used his death to halt the program permanently, and by the late 1470s the Ministry of War had reportedly destroyed the navigation records to prevent future emperors from reviving the voyages.

The voyages were a project of the eunuch faction, fought against by the Confucian official class on grounds of cost and agrarian-priority ideology; when the Yongle Emperor died and his son sided with the Confucian opposition, there was no commercial constituency capable of sustaining the project without imperial sponsorship.
China would not project naval power on a comparable scale for almost six hundred years, until the People's Republic began building a blue-water fleet in the 2000s.

title: The Eunuch Empire
subtitle: Wang Zhen, the Tumu Crisis, the Great Wall rebuilt, and the autopilot empire of the mid-Ming
bullets: The first eunuch to dominate Ming politics outright was Wang Zhen, who as chief eunuch persuaded the twenty-two-year-old Zhengtong Emperor to lead a personal punitive expedition against the Mongols in 1449 and ended his career when the Mongol leader Esen Taishi destroyed the army at the Tumu fortress.

On September 1, 1449, Esen Taishi ambushed and destroyed a Ming army of perhaps 500,000 men at the Tumu Crisis, capturing the Zhengtong Emperor himself — the worst Chinese military defeat since the Mongol conquest and the first capture of a Chinese emperor since the Mongol Yuan.

Yu Qian, the Vice Minister of War, organized the defense of Beijing during the Tumu Crisis, refused ransom demands, enthroned the captured emperor's brother as the Jingtai Emperor, and held off Esen's siege; he was later executed on trumped-up treason charges when Zhengtong returned to power.

The Great Wall of China that tourists visit today is almost entirely Ming construction — over 5,000 miles of stone and brick built between 1450 and 1600 in response to the strategic lessons of Tumu, with construction effort employing millions of conscripted laborers across more than a century.

The Wall did not actually stop Mongol raids — Altan Khan invaded northern China in 1550 and besieged Beijing for several days — and it failed completely against the Manchus in 1644 when Wu Sangui opened the Shanhaiguan gate from the inside.

The chief eunuch Liu Jin, who ran the Ming government from 1505 to 1510 under the eccentric Zhengde Emperor, accumulated personal wealth estimated at 250 million ounces of silver — possibly the largest individual fortune in pre-modern world history.

The Grand Canal economy moved up to four million bushels of grain per year north from the Yangtze delta to feed Beijing, with the Yangtze cities Suzhou, Hangzhou, Yangzhou, and Wuxi becoming some of the wealthiest urban centers in the world during the mid-Ming.

The mid-Ming was the autopilot empire — the institutions Hongwu built ran on their own momentum, with the eunuch bureaucracy filling the coordination role that the abolished Chief Minister had previously occupied, until external stresses in the late sixteenth century broke the system.

title: Single Whip and the Silver Globalization
subtitle: Zhang Juzheng's reforms, the silver flood from Mexico and Japan, and the Wanli Emperor's thirty-year work strike
bullets: Grand Secretary Zhang Juzheng ran the Ming government as effective regent during the early reign of the boy Wanli Emperor between 1572 and 1582, conducting fiscal consolidation, comprehensive land surveys, and the implementation of the Single Whip Tax Reform that briefly stabilized the dynasty's finances.

The Single Whip Tax Reform consolidated the Ming's labyrinthine fiscal system into a single annual silver payment per landholding, replacing a patchwork of grain levies and corvée labor obligations and tying the entire Chinese economy to global silver flows.

After 1581 every rural household across the most populous country on Earth needed silver to pay their taxes; Chinese silver mines could not produce nearly enough metal, and the shortfall had to be made up by imports from Spanish America via Manila and from Japan via Macao.

By the early seventeenth century perhaps 10,000 tons of silver had flowed into China — possibly a third or more of all silver mined in the early-modern world — making Ming China the eastern pole of the global silver economy whose western pole was the Spanish Habsburg empire.

The Wanli Emperor reigned for forty-eight years, the longest of any Ming ruler, but spent the last thirty in self-imposed seclusion after a succession dispute, refusing to attend court, sign documents, or fill vacant offices — the most famous case of an early-modern monarchy operating on autopilot for decades.

Forensic analysis of Wanli's skeleton excavated from the Dingling tomb in 1956 showed he had been physically incapacitated for much of his later reign, with one leg significantly shorter than the other from a chronic condition that would have made standing in heavy ceremonial robes extremely painful.

The Italian Jesuit Matteo Ricci reached Beijing in 1601 after twenty years of mastering classical Chinese, dressing as a Confucian scholar, and translating European mathematics into Chinese — the first European granted permanent residence at the Ming court and the most sophisticated cross-cultural intellectual encounter of the early-modern world.

Ricci's collaborator Xu Guangqi, a Grand Secretary who became one of the highest-ranking Catholic converts in Ming China, helped translate the first six books of Euclid's Elements into Chinese in 1607 and championed European astronomical methods at the Beijing observatory.

title: The Imjin War
subtitle: Hideyoshi's invasion of Korea, the seven-year Ming intervention, and the bankruptcy that opened the door to the Manchus
bullets: In April 1592, Toyotomi Hideyoshi — the unifier of Japan after the Sengoku period of civil wars — landed an invasion army of 158,000 men at Busan with the stated goal of conquering Ming China through Korea as the staging route.
Korea collapsed almost immediately: Busan fell on the first day, Seoul within three weeks, Pyongyang by June; the Korean court fled to the Yalu and sent desperate appeals to Beijing.

The Ming dispatched expeditionary armies that recaptured Pyongyang in January 1593 and Seoul in April 1593, pushing the Japanese back to a defensive perimeter on the southern coast and forcing the truce that lasted from 1593 to 1597.

The Korean admiral Yi Sun-sin's geobukseon turtle ships — armored galleys with iron-spiked roofs and multiple cannons — defeated successive Japanese fleets through tactical brilliance, including the Battle of Hansando in 1592 and the Battle of Myeongnyang in 1597 where 13 Korean ships defeated a Japanese fleet of perhaps 333.

The negotiations between the truce phases collapsed in 1596 when the Ming embassy arrived at Hideyoshi's court carrying an imperial seal investing Hideyoshi as 'King of Japan' — a tributary status he considered an enormous insult — because both negotiators had been lying to their own governments about what the other side had agreed to.

The second Japanese invasion of 1597 produced the Mound of Ears at Kyoto, assembled from the noses of perhaps 38,000 Korean and Chinese dead — Japanese commanders were required to send back ear-or-nose proof of kills since heads were too heavy to ship across the strait.

The war killed roughly two million people total, devastated Korea so thoroughly that the country took most of the seventeenth century to recover, and ended only when Hideyoshi died in August 1598 and the Council of Five Regents ordered the Japanese armies to withdraw.

The Ming spent roughly 20 to 26 million ounces of silver on the campaigns, effectively bankrupting the late-Wanli treasury and forcing cuts to northern frontier defense at exactly the moment the Manchu confederation under Nurhaci was beginning to consolidate.

title: The Late Ming Cultural Peak
subtitle: the four great vernacular novels, Wang Yangming's philosophy, Donglin reform, and Ming porcelain as the global luxury good
bullets: Romance of the Three Kingdoms reached its standard form in the Mao Lun edition of 1522 — 800,000 words across 120 chapters — establishing the conventions of Chinese historical fiction and remaining one of the most widely read Chinese books of all time.

Water Margin, attributed to Shi Nai'an and reaching standard form in the Ming, tells the story of 108 outlaws who form a bandit fellowship at Liangshan Marsh; Mao Zedong reportedly identified strongly with several of its characters.

Journey to the West, published in 1592 and attributed to Wu Cheng'en, retells the seventh-century pilgrimage of the Tang monk Xuanzang to India accompanied by the trickster Monkey King Sun Wukong, the lustful pig demon Zhu Bajie, and the river spirit Sha Wujing — the most beloved of the four great novels.

Plum in the Golden Vase, published anonymously in the early seventeenth century, was the first Chinese novel of explicit sexual realism and was banned in China for most of the period from the 1620s to the 1980s, with full unexpurgated editions still relatively recent in the People's Republic.

Wang Yangming, the most influential Chinese philosopher of the Ming, taught that moral knowledge was innate to every person and that the unity of knowing and acting was the key to ethical life; his ideas spread to Korea and to Japan, where as Yomeigaku they shaped the Meiji Restoration reformers.

The Donglin Society was a moralistic neo-Confucian reform faction founded in 1604 that attacked court corruption and eunuch power; Wei Zhongxian's secret police tortured many leading Donglin scholars to death in the 1625–1626 purge, including Yang Lian who was tortured for forty-five days before he died.

Ming blue-and-white porcelain from the imperial kilns at Jingdezhen became the most globally traded luxury good of the early-modern world, with the Dutch East India Company alone importing perhaps 200,000 pieces per year by the 1620s.

Dutch potters at Delft attempted to copy Ming porcelain for European households who could not afford the real thing — the iconic Delft Blue ware was originally a deliberate imitation of Chinese export goods, a remarkable case of European craft tradition founded on reproducing Chinese originals.

title: The Manchu Conquest
subtitle: Wei Zhongxian, the Manchu rise, Li Zicheng's rebellion, the Chongzhen Emperor's suicide on Coal Hill, and the Qing founding
bullets: Wei Zhongxian — a minor palace eunuch who had reportedly castrated himself in adulthood to escape gambling debts — rose under the boy Tianqi Emperor to control the Directorate of Ceremonial and the Eastern Depot secret police, becoming the most powerful eunuch dictator in Ming history from 1620 to 1627.

Wei organized something no previous Chinese eunuch had attempted: a personal cult, with provincial officials erecting shrines to the living chief eunuch as a quasi-divine figure, some placing his statue alongside or in front of statues of Confucius.

Nurhaci unified the Jurchen tribes between the 1580s and 1610s, invented a written script for Manchu, organized his fighters into the Eight Banners, and proclaimed the Later Jin dynasty in 1616; his son Hong Taiji renamed the people Manchu in 1635 and the dynasty Qing in 1636.

Li Zicheng, a former Shaanxi post-station courier thrown out of work by Ming budget cuts, became the leader of the largest peasant rebellion of the late Ming, swept across northern China through the 1630s and 1640s, and captured Beijing in April 1644.

On the morning of April 25, 1644, the Chongzhen Emperor walked out of the Forbidden City with one eunuch attendant, climbed Coal Hill behind the palace, and hanged himself from a pagoda tree, leaving a suicide note blaming his ministers — 'All my ministers have failed me.'

The Ming general Wu Sangui, commanding the elite Liaodong army at the Shanhaiguan pass, faced an impossible choice and chose the Manchus; on May 27, 1644, the combined Wu-Manchu army crushed Li Zicheng at the Battle of Shanhai Pass and the Manchu prince-regent Dorgon entered Beijing six days later.

Southern Ming loyalist regimes continued resistance for nearly two decades — the Hongguang regime at Nanjing fell 1645, the Longwu regime in Fujian fell 1646, the Yongli regime fled into Burma where the emperor was captured and executed in 1662 — and Koxinga expelled the Dutch from Taiwan in 1661 to establish a Ming-loyalist state.

What survived from the Ming was extensive: Beijing as the imperial capital, the Forbidden City, the Great Wall, the four great novels, Ming blue-and-white porcelain, the examination tradition, the cultural template against which every later Chinese regime measured itself — the dynasty died in 1644 and China is still living in its ruins.
